title: NASC-seq2 Protocol
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"dji9v","text":"Insights into transcriptional bursting kinetics and regulation have emerged from real-time nascent RNA imaging and analyses of static RNA counts over cells. Here, we developed sensitive single-cell profiling of newly transcribed (or new) RNA in cells (NASC-seq2) that can easily be applied to tens of thousands of single cells to help shed new light on bursting dynamics and coordination.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: Prepare the following lysis buffer on ice. Reagent Final concentration 1 reaction 384-well plate 1 Recombinant RNAse Inhibitor (40 U/uL) 2.5 u/uL 0.0188 uL 8.66 uL 2 4sU containing spUMI pool (OPTIONAL, 0.01 ng/uL) 0.04 pg/uL 0.0012 uL 0.553 uL 3 Triton-X100 (2 %) 0.1% 0.015 uL 6.91 uL 4 Nuclease-free water - 0.265 uL 122.1 uL 5 Total 0.3 uL 138.2 uL On ice
Use a nanodispenser to distribute 0.3 uL of freshly prepared lysis buffer to each well of vapor-lock containing 384-well PCR plate. 0.3 µL, Lysis buffer On ice
Optional: Store lysis plate. If not immediately continuing with the next step, prepared lysis plates can be sealed with aluminium seals and stored at -80°C.

Grow cells in the presence of 50 μM 4sU. It can be helpful to collect untreated cells as a control. 4sU is light-sensitive, and direct light (i.e. light in the cell culture hood, etc) should be reduced to a minimum. Do not refreeze leftovers from 4sU aliquot. Labeling times can vary for different celltypes and biological applications, but in general, short labeling times (i.e. less than 30 minutes) may result in poor separation of new and old molecules. Depending on your downstream analysis, this may affect your results.
Stop the labeling by transferring your cells to 15-ml falcon tubes on ice and wash them with cold PBS. On ice
Distribute single cells into each well of the lysis plate by FACS sorting.
Spin down and seal the plates with aluminium seals and store them at -80°C.

Prepare the alkylation mix at room temperature. Reagent Final concentration 1 reaction 384-well plate 1 Tris-HCL (pH 8.4, 1 M) 50 mM 0.03 uL 13.8 uL 2 DMSO (100 %) 45 % 0.24 uL 110.6 uL 3 IAA in DMSO (200 mM) 10 mM 0.03 uL 13.8 uL 4 Total 0.3 uL 138.2 uL Please note that the calculations in this mix are calculated to the final volume in the alkylation reaction (i.e. 0.6uL). The DMSO final concentration listed above also accounts for the DMSO that is added with the IAA. Room temperature To avoid problems with iodocatamide stability and variability of alkylating potential, we use single-use vials (Sigma A3221-10VL) of iodoacetamide that are dissolved in DMSO at 200mM right before they are used to prepare the alkylation mix above and the remainder is discarded. Iodoacetamide should be handled in a fume hood and in accordence with local environmental health and safety regulations.
Using a nanodispenser, distribute 0.3 uL of freshly prepared alkylation mix to all wells of the 384-well plate containing cells and lysis-buffer.
Spin the 384-well plate down at >3,000 x G for 10 seconds.
Incubate the plate at 50C for 15 minutes. While this is running, prepare the quenching mix (see the next section). 50 °C, 15 minutes

Prepare the quencing mix on ice. Reagent Final concentration 1 reaction 384-well plate 1 DTT (1 M in H2O) 35 mM 0.035 uL 16.13 uL 2 dNTPs (10 mM each) 0.5 mM each 0.2 uL 92.16 uL 3 Oligo-dT (100 uM) 0.6 uM 0.024 uL 11.06 uL 4 Recombinant RNAse Inhibitor (40 U/uL) 0.4 u/uL 0.04 uL 18.43 uL 5 Nuclease-free water - 0.101 uL 46.54 uL 6 Total 0.4 uL 184.32 Note that the dNTPs, oligo-dT and RRI concentrations above are calculated to the final volume in the Reverse Transcription mix (4uL) and not the Quenching mix (1uL).
Spin down at >3,000 G for 10 seconds. 3000 x g, °C, 10 seconds
Using a nanodispenser, distribute 0.4 uL of freshly prepared quenching and denuturation mix to all wells of the 384-well plate.
Spin the 384-well plate down at >3,000 x G for 10 seconds
Incubate the plate for 5 minutes at room temperature, followed by 10 minutes at 72C and a final hold at 4C. While this is running, prepare the Reverse Transcription mix (see the next section)

Prepare the Reverse Transcription mix as below Reagent Final concentration 1 reaction 384-well plate 1 Tris-HCL (1 M, pH 8.0) 25 mM 0,1 uL 46.08 uL 2 NaCl (1 M) 35 mM 0,14 uL 64.51 uL 3 GTP (100 mM) 1 mM 0,04 uL 18.43 uL 4 MgCl2 (1 M) 2.5 mM 0.01 uL 4.61 uL 5 PEG (40 %) 5 % 0.5 uL 230.4 uL 6 DTT (1 M) 2 mM + carry-over from quenching and denaturation mix 0.008 uL 3.69 uL 7 Recombinant RNAse Inhibitor (40 U/uL) 0.4 U/uL + carry-over from lysis as well as quenching and denaturation mix 0.04 uL 18.43 uL 8 Template Switching Oligo (1 mM) 2 uM 0.008 uL 3.69 uL 9 Maxima H-minus RT enzyme (200 U/uL) 2 U/uL 0.04 uL 18.43 uL 10 H2O - 2.11 uL 974.13 uL 11 Total 3 uL 1382.4 uL
Dispense 3 uL of the freshly prepared Reverse Transcription mix to all wells of the 384-well plate.
Spin the 384-well plate down at >3,000 x G for 10 seconds
Place the plate in the thermal cycler and start the Reverse Transcription program

For the remainer of the protocol we are using standard Smart-seq3 reaction conditions. Hagemann-Jensen M, Ziegenhain C, Chen P, Ramsköld D, Hendriks GJ, Larsson AJM, Faridani OR, Sandberg R. A detailed description of the Smart-seq3 protocol is also available on protocols.io. Smart-seq3 Protocol by Michael Hagemann-Jensen For convenience, the preamplification PCR protocol is also included below.

Prepare the preamplification PCR mix as below Reagent Final concentration 1 reaction 384-well plate 1 Kapa HiFi HotStart buffer (5 X) 1 X 2.0 uL 820 uL 2 dNTPs (25 mM/each) 0.3 mM/each 0.12 uL 49.2 uL 3 MgCl2 (100 mM) 0.5 mM 0.05 uL 20.5 uL 4 Fwd Primer (100 uM) 0.5 uM 0.05 uL 20.5 uL 5 Rev Primer (100 uM) 0.1 uM 0.01 uL 4.1 uL 6 Polymerase (1 U/uL) 0.02 U/uL 0.2 uL 82 uL 7 Nuclease Free Water - 3.57 uL 1463.7 uL 8 Total 6 uL 2460 uL Preamplification PCR mix from Hagemann-Jensen et al.
Dispense 6 uL of the freshly prepared preamplification PCR mix to all wells of the 384-well plate.
Spin the 384-well plate down at >3,000 x G for 10 seconds

Place the plate in the thermal cycler and start the following PCR program A B C D 1 Step Temperature Time Cycles 2 Initial denaturation 98 °C 3 min 1x 3 Denaturation 98 °C 20 sec 20-25x 4 Annealing 65°C 30 sec 5 Elongation 72 °C 4 min 6 Final Elongation 72 °C 5 min 1x 7 Hold 4 °C Hold �

For details on the purification of the amplified cDNA, the quality control and the tagmentation to produce the final sequencing libraries, please refer to the Smart-seq3 protocols.io. Smart-seq3 Protocol by Michael Hagemann-Jensen

We have chosen to perform relatively long short-read sequencing with most data produced for NASC-seq2 being sequenced PE200. We highly recommend this as it will increase your ability to call individual molecules as either new (labeled) or old (unlabeled). For details on data processing and analysis, please see the lab github: https://github.com/sandberg-lab/NASC-seq2

Using an automated pipetting platform or multichannel pipette, dispense 3uL of Vapor-Lock (Qiagen) to each well of a 384-well plate. Note: Do not use a non-contact dispenser (such as Dispendix IDOT or Formulatrix Mantis) for this step as the Vapor-Lock may severely damage the dispenser. We do this using an Agilent Bravo platform.
creator: Gert-Jan Hendriks
